topic: Someone discovers their teenager has a secret social media account
raw_output:
[story 1]

I found the login details tucked inside a seldom-used charging case. My hands started trembling as I typed them in, a slow, agonizing crawl across the screen. The profile picture that loaded wasn't the carefully curated image of my daughter; it was a blurry selfie taken in a dimly lit room, surrounded by neon lights. A wave of heat washed over my face, making my cheeks flush crimson. When she walked in, whistling a tune, I nearly dropped the phone. The silence stretched, thick and suffocating, broken only by the frantic thumping in my own chest. "What are you doing?" Her voice was sharp, instantly shifting the atmosphere from quiet dread to outright confrontation. I could only stare at the screen, my mouth dry, unable to form a coherent response.

[story 2]

It was the notification sound that did it—a familiar chime from a platform I didn't recognize. Curiosity, sharp and unwelcome, compelled me to investigate. The account was active, filled with posts that were jarringly different from the persona my teenager presented to the world. Every caption felt like a carefully constructed lie, and I felt a tightening sensation in my throat. I backed away from the computer, my feet shuffling on the hardwood floor. When my daughter entered the room, she paused at the doorway, her expression unreadable. I quickly minimized the window, my gaze fixed firmly on a spot on the wall, wishing the floor would swallow me whole. "Just... checking something," I managed, the words sounding thin and brittle.

[story 3]

The evidence was undeniable: a password she’d whispered under her breath last week. Opening the account felt like stepping into a crowded, unwanted party. The stream of images and short videos hit me in a sudden, overwhelming rush. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I felt a distinct prickling sensation behind my eyes, a desperate urge to disappear. When she finally appeared, leaning against the doorframe, I felt every muscle in my body tense up. I quickly closed the browser tab, shoving the laptop away as if it were suddenly radioactive. I cleared my throat, trying to project calm, but my voice cracked slightly. "Nothing important," I mumbled, focusing intently on straightening a throw pillow, desperate for the moment to pass.
